topics.title: Solving Trigonometric Equations with Identities

definitions.text: DefinitionS: IDENTITIES Pythagorean Identities \[\cos ^{2} (t)+\sin ^{2} (t)=1\quad 1+\cot ^{2} (t)=\csc ^{2} (t)\quad 1+\tan ^{2} (t)=\sec ^{2} (t)\] Negative Angle Identities \[\sin (-t)=-\sin (t)\quad \cos (-t)=\cos (t)\quad \tan (-t)=-\tan (t)\] \[\csc (-t)=-\csc (t)\quad \sec (-t)=\sec (t)\quad \cot (-t)=-\cot (t)\] Reciprocal Identities \[\sec (t)=\dfrac{1}{\cos (t)}\quad \csc (t)=\dfrac{1}{\sin (t)}\quad \tan (t)=\dfrac{\sin (t)}{\cos (t)}\quad \cot (t)=\dfrac{1}{\tan (t)}\]

examples.text:
Example \(\PageIndex{3}\) Solve \(2\sin ^{2} (t)-\cos (t)=1\) for all solutions with \(0\le t<2\pi\). Solution Since this equation has a mix of sine and cosine functions, it becomes more complicated to solve. It is usually easier to work with an equation involving only one trig function. This is where we can use the Pythagorean Identity. \[2\sin ^{2} (t)-\cos (t)=1
onumber\]Using \(\sin ^{2} (	heta )=1-\cos ^{2} (	heta )\) \[2\left(1-\cos ^{2} (t)ight)-\cos (t)=1
onumber\]Distributing the 2 \[2-2\cos ^{2} (t)-\cos (t)=1
onumber\] Since this is now quadratic in cosine, we rearrange the equation so one side is zero and factor. \[-2\cos ^{2} (t)-\cos (t)+1=0
onumber\]Multiply by -1 to simplify the factoring \[2\cos ^{2} (t)+\cos (t)-1=0
onumber\]Factor \[\left(2\cos (t)-1ight)\left(\cos (t)+1ight)=0
onumber\] This product will be zero if either factor is zero, so we can break this into two separate cases and solve each independently. \[2\cos (t)-1=0	ext{ or }\cos (t)+1=0
onumber\] \[\cos (t)=\dfrac{1}{2}	ext{ or }\cos (t)=-1
onumber\] \[t=\dfrac{\pi }{3}	ext{ or }t=\dfrac{5\pi }{3}	ext{ or }t=\pi
onumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{4}\) Solve \(	an (x)=3\sin (x)\) for all solutions with \(0\le x<2\pi\). Solution With a combination of tangent and sine, we might try rewriting tangent \[	an (x)=3\sin (x)
onumber\] \[\dfrac{\sin (x)}{\cos (x)} =3\sin (x)
onumber\]Multiplying both sides by cosine \[\sin (x)=3\sin (x)\cos (x) 
onumber\] At this point, you may be tempted to divide both sides of the equation by sin(\(x\)). Resist the urge . When we divide both sides of an equation by a quantity, we are assuming the quantity is never zero. In this case, when sin(\(x\)) = 0 the equation is satisfied, so we’d lose those solutions if we divided by the sine. To avoid this problem, we can rearrange the equation so that one side is zero (You technically can divide by sin( x ), as long as you separately consider the case where sin( x ) = 0. Since it is easy to forget this step, the factoring approach used in the example is recommended.). \[\sin (x)-3\sin (x)\cos (x)=0
onumber\]Factoring out sin(\(x\)) from both parts \[\sin (x)\left(1-3\cos (x)ight)=0 
onumber\] From here, we can see we get solutions when \(\sin (x)=0\) or \(1-3\cos (x)=0\). Using our knowledge of the special angles of the unit circle, \[\sin (x)=0	ext{ when }x = 0	ext{ or }x = \pi
onumber\] For the second equation, we will need the inverse cosine. \[1-3\cos (x)=0
onumber\] \[\cos (x)=\dfrac{1}{3}
onumber\]Using our calculator or technology \[x=\cos ^{-1} \left(\dfrac{1}{3} ight)\approx 1.231
onumber\]Using symmetry to find a second solution \[x=2\pi -1.231=5.052 
onumber\] We have four solutions on \(0 \le x<2\pi\): \[x = 0, 1.231, \quad\pi , 5.052
onumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{5}\) Solve \(\dfrac{4}{\sec ^{2} (	heta )} +3\cos \left(	heta ight)=2\cot \left(	heta ight)	an \left(	heta ight)\) for all solutions with \(0\le 	heta <2\pi\). Solution \[\dfrac{4}{\sec ^{2} (	heta )} +3\cos \left(	heta ight)=2\cot \left(	heta ight)	an \left(	heta ight)
onumber\] Using the reciprocal identities \[4\cos ^{2} (	heta )+3\cos (	heta )=2\dfrac{1}{	an (	heta )} 	an (	heta )
onumber\] Simplifying \[4\cos ^{2} \left(	heta ight)+3\cos \left(	heta ight)=2
onumber\] Subtracting 2 from each side \[4\cos ^{2} \left(	heta ight)+3\cos \left(	heta ight)-2=0
onumber\] This does not appear to factor nicely so we use the quadratic formula, remembering that we are solving for cos( \(	heta\) ). \[\cos (	heta )=\dfrac{-3\pm \sqrt{3^{2} -4(4)(-2)} }{2(4)} =\dfrac{-3\pm \sqrt{41} }{8}
onumber\] Using the negative square root first, \[\cos (	heta )=\dfrac{-3-\sqrt{41} }{8} =-1.175
onumber\] This has no solutions, since the cosine can’t be less than -1. Using the positive square root, \[\cos (	heta )=\dfrac{-3+\sqrt{41} }{8} =0.425
onumber\] \[	heta =\cos ^{-1} \left(0.425ight)=1.131
onumber\] By symmetry, a second solution can be found \[	heta =2\pi -1.131=5.152
onumber\]

topics.description: In the last chapter, we solved basic trigonometric equations. In this section, we explore the techniques needed to solve more complicated trig equations.

topics.title: Section 7.2 Addition and Subtraction Identities

examples.text:
Example \(\PageIndex{2}\) Rewrite \(\sin \left(x-\dfrac{\pi }{4} ight)\) in terms of sin(\(x\)) and cos(\(x\)). Solution \[\sin \left(x-\dfrac{\pi }{4} ight)
onumber\]Use the difference of angles identity for sine \[=\sin \left(xight)\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi }{4} ight)-\cos \left(xight)\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{4} ight)
onumber\] Evaluate the cosine and sine and rearrange \[=\dfrac{\sqrt{2} }{2} \sin \left(xight)-\dfrac{\sqrt{2} }{2} \cos \left(xight)
onumber\]

topics.description: In this section, we begin expanding our repertoire of trigonometric identities. The sum and difference identities \[\cos (\alpha -\beta )=\cos (\alpha )\cos (\beta )+\sin (\alpha )\sin (\beta )\] \[\cos (\alpha +\beta )=\cos (\alpha )\cos (\beta )-\sin (\alpha )\sin (\beta )\] \[

topics.title: Combining Waves of Equal Period
topics.body_text:
A sinusoidal function of the form \(f(x)=A\sin (Bx+C)\) can be rewritten using the sum of angles identity. Notice that the result is a stretch of the sine added to a different stretch of the cosine, but both have the same horizontal compression, which results in the same period. We might ask now whether this process can be reversed – can a combination of a sine and cosine of the same period be written as a single sinusoidal function? To explore this, we will look in general at the procedure used in the example above. \[f(x)=A\sin (Bx+C)
onumber\]Use the sum of angles identity \[=A\left(\sin (Bx)\cos (C)+\cos (Bx)\sin (C)ight)
onumber\]Distribute the \(A\) \[=A\sin (Bx)\cos (C)+A\cos (Bx)\sin (C)
onumber\]Rearrange the terms a bit \[=A\cos (C)\sin (Bx)+A\sin (C)\cos (Bx)
onumber\] Based on this result, if we have an expression of the form \(m\sin (Bx)+n\cos (Bx)\), we could rewrite it as a single sinusoidal function if we can find values A and C so that \(m\sin (Bx)+n\cos (Bx)\) \(=A\cos (C)\sin (Bx)+A\sin (C)\cos (Bx)\), which will require that: \[ egin{array}{l} {m=A\cos (C)} \ {n=A\sin (C)} \end{array}
onumber\] which can be rewritten as \[ egin{array}{l} {\dfrac{m}{A} =\cos (C)} \ {\dfrac{n}{A} =\sin (C)} \end{array}
onumber\] To find \(A\), \[m^{2} +n^{2} =\left(A\cos (C)ight)^{2} +\left(A\sin (C)ight)^{2}
onumber\] \[=A^{2} \cos ^{2} (C)+A^{2} \sin ^{2} (C)
onumber\] \[=A^{2} \left(\cos ^{2} (C)+\sin ^{2} (C)ight)
onumber\]Apply the Pythagorean Identity and simplify \[=A^{2}
onumber\] REWRITING A SUM OF SINE AND COSINE AS A SINGLE SINE To rewrite \(m\sin (Bx)+n\cos (Bx)\) as \(A\sin (Bx+C)\) \[A^{2} =m^{2} +n^{2}\quad \cos (C)=\dfrac{m}{A}	ext{ and }\sin (C)=\dfrac{n}{A}\] You can use either of the last two equations to solve for possible values of C . Since there will usually be two possible solutions, we will need to look at both to determine which quadrant C is in and determine which solution for C satisfies both equations. Exercise \(\PageIndex{3}\) Rewrite \(-3\sqrt{2} \sin (5x)+3\sqrt{2} \cos (5x)\) as a single sinusoidal function. \[A^{2} =\left(-3\sqrt{2} ight)^{2} +\left(3\sqrt{2} ight)^{2} =36\quad A=6
onumber\] \[\cos (C)=\dfrac{-3\sqrt{2} }{6} =\dfrac{-\sqrt{2} }{2}\quad \sin (C)=\dfrac{3\sqrt{2} }{6} =\dfrac{\sqrt{2} }{2}\quad C=\dfrac{3\pi }{4}
onumber\] \[6\sin \left(5x+\dfrac{3\pi }{4} ight)
onumber\] Rewriting a combination of sine and cosine of equal periods as a single sinusoidal function provides an approach for solving some equations. The Product-to-Sum and Sum-to-Product Identities The Product-to-Sum Identities \[ egin{array}{l} {\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\sin (\alpha + eta )+\sin (\alpha - eta )ight)} \ {\sin (\alpha )\sin ( eta )=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos (\alpha - eta )-\cos (\alpha + eta )ight)} \ {\cos (\alpha )\cos ( eta )=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos (\alpha + eta )+\cos (\alpha - eta )ight)} \end{array}\] We will prove the first of these, using the sum and difference of angles identities from the beginning of the section. The proofs of the other two identities are similar and are left as an exercise. Proof of the product-to-sum identity for sin(\(\alpha\))cos(\( eta\)) Recall the sum and difference of angles identities from earlier \[\sin (\alpha + eta )=\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )+\cos (\alpha )\sin ( eta )
onumber\] \[\sin (\alpha - eta )=\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )-\cos (\alpha )\sin ( eta )
onumber\] Adding these two equations, we obtain \[\sin (\alpha + eta )+\sin (\alpha - eta )=2\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )
onumber\] Dividing by 2, we establish the identity \[\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\sin (\alpha + eta )+\sin (\alpha - eta )ight)
onumber\] Exercise \(\PageIndex{4}\) Evaluate \(\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi }{12} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi }{12} ight)\). \[\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi }{12} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi }{12} ight)=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi }{12} +\dfrac{\pi }{12} ight)+\cos \left(\dfrac{11\pi }{12} -\dfrac{\pi }{12} ight)ight)
onumber\] \[=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos \left(\pi ight)+\cos \left(\dfrac{5\pi }{6} ight)ight)=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(-1-\dfrac{\sqrt{3} }{2} ight)
onumber\] \[=\dfrac{-2-\sqrt{3} }{4}
onumber\] The Sum-to-Product Identities \[\sin \left(uight)+\sin \left(vight)=2\sin \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)\] \[\sin \left(uight)-\sin \left(vight)=2\sin \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\] \[\cos \left(uight)+\cos \left(vight)=2\cos \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)\] \[\cos \left(uight)-\cos \left(vight)=-2\sin \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\sin \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)\] We will again prove one of these and leave the rest as an exercise. Proof of the sum-to-product identity for sine function We define two new variables: \[ egin{array}{l} {u=\alpha + eta } \ {v=\alpha - eta } \end{array}
onumber\] Adding these equations yields \(u+v=2\alpha\), giving \(\alpha =\dfrac{u+v}{2}\) Subtracting the equations yields \(u-v=2 eta\), or \( eta =\dfrac{u-v}{2}\) Substituting these expressions into the product-to-sum identity \[\sin (\alpha )\cos ( eta )=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\sin (\alpha + eta )+\sin (\alpha - eta )ight)
onumber\]gives \[\sin \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\sin \left(uight)+\sin \left(vight)ight)
onumber\]Multiply by 2 on both sides \[2\sin \left(\dfrac{u+v}{2} ight)\cos \left(\dfrac{u-v}{2} ight)=\sin \left(uight)+\sin \left(vight)
onumber\]Establishing the identity Exercise \(\PageIndex{5}\) Notice that, using the negative angle identity, \(\sin \left(uight)-\sin \left(vight)=\sin (u)+\sin (-v)\). Use this along with the sum of sines identity to prove the sum-to-product identity for \(\sin \left(uight)-\sin \left(vight)\). \[\sin (u)-\sin (v)
onumber\]Use negative angle identity for sine \[\sin (u) + \sin (-v)
onumber\]Use sum-to-product identity for sine \[2	ext{sin} (\dfrac{u + (-v)}{2}) 	ext{cos} (\dfrac{u - (-v)}{2})
onumber\]Eliminate the parenthesis \[2	ext{sin} (\dfrac{u - v}{2}) 	ext{cos} (\dfrac{u + v}{2})
onumber\]Establishing the identity

examples.text:
Example \(\PageIndex{5}\) Rewrite \(f(x)=4\sin \left(3x+\dfrac{\pi }{3} ight)\) as a sum of sine and cosine. Solution \[4\sin \left(3x+\dfrac{\pi }{3} ight)
onumber\]Using the sum of angles identity \[=4\left(\sin \left(3xight)\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi }{3} ight)+\cos \left(3xight)\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{3} ight)ight)
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and cosine \[=4\left(\sin \left(3xight)\cdot \dfrac{1}{2} +\cos \left(3xight)\cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3} }{2} ight)
onumber\]Distribute and simplify \[=2\sin \left(3xight)+2\sqrt{3} \cos \left(3xight)
onumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{6}\) Rewrite \(4\sqrt{3} \sin (2x)-4\cos (2x)\) as a single sinusoidal function. Solution Using the formulas above, \(A^{2} =\left(4\sqrt{3} \right)^{2} +\left(-4\right)^{2} =16\cdot 3+16=64\), so \(A = 8\). Solving for \(C\), \[\cos (C)=\dfrac{4\sqrt{3} }{8} =\dfrac{\sqrt{3} }{2}\text{ so }C=\dfrac{\pi }{6}\text{ or }C=\dfrac{11\pi }{6}\nonumber\] However, notice \(\sin (C)=\dfrac{-4}{8} =-\dfrac{1}{2}\). Sine is negative in the third and fourth quadrant, so the angle that works for both is \(C=\dfrac{11\pi }{6}\). Combining these results gives us the expression \[8\sin \left(2x+\dfrac{11\pi }{6} \right)\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{8}\) Write \(\sin (2t)\sin (4t)\) as a sum or difference. Solution Using the product-to-sum identity for a product of sines \[\sin (2t)\sin (4t)=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos (2t-4t)-\cos (2t+4t)ight)
onumber\] \[=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos (-2t)-\cos (6t)ight)
onumber\]If desired, apply the negative angle identity \[=\dfrac{1}{2} \left(\cos (2t)-\cos (6t)ight)
onumber\]Distribute \[=\dfrac{1}{2} \cos (2t)-\dfrac{1}{2} \cos (6t)
onumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{9}\) Evaluate \(\cos (15{}^\circ )-\cos (75{}^\circ )\). Solution Using the sum-to-product identity for the difference of cosines, \[\cos (15{}^\circ )-\cos (75{}^\circ )
onumber\] \[=-2\sin \left(\dfrac{15{}^\circ +75{}^\circ }{2} ight)\sin \left(\dfrac{15{}^\circ -75{}^\circ }{2} ight)
onumber\]Simplify \[=-2\sin \left(45{}^\circ ight)\sin \left(-30{}^\circ ight)
onumber\]Evaluate \[=-2\cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2} }{2} \cdot \dfrac{-1}{2} =\dfrac{\sqrt{2} }{2}
onumber\]

topics.description: A sinusoidal function of the form \(f(x)=A\sin (Bx+C)\) can be rewritten using the sum of angles identity. Notice that the result is a stretch of the sine added to a different stretch of the cosine, but both have the same horizontal compression, which results in the same period.

topics.title: Double Angle Identities

examples.text:
Example \(\PageIndex{2}\) Simplify the expressions a) \(2\cos ^{2} \left(12{}^\circ ight)-1\) b) \(8\sin \left(3xight)\cos \left(3xight)\) Solution a) Notice that the expression is in the same form as one version of the double angle identity for cosine: \(\cos (2	heta )=2\cos ^{2} (	heta )-1\). Using this, \[2\cos ^{2} \left(12{}^\circ ight)-1=\cos \left(2\cdot 12{}^\circ ight)=\cos \left(24{}^\circ ight)
onumber\] b) This expression looks similar to the result of the double angle identity for sine. \[8\sin \left(3xight)\cos \left(3xight)
onumber\]Factoring a 4 out of the original expression \[4\cdot 2\sin \left(3xight)\cos \left(3xight)
onumber\]Applying the double angle identity \[4\sin (6x)
onumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{5}\) Solve \(\cos (2t)=\cos (t)\) for all solutions with \(0\le t<2\pi\). Solution In general when solving trig equations, it makes things more complicated when we have a mix of sines and cosines and when we have a mix of functions with different periods. In this case, we can use a double angle identity to rewrite the cos(2 t ). When choosing which form of the double angle identity to use, we notice that we have a cosine on the right side of the equation. We try to limit our equation to one trig function, which we can do by choosing the version of the double angle formula for cosine that only involves cosine. \[\cos (2t)=\cos (t)
onumber\]Apply the double angle identity \[2\cos ^{2} (t)-1=\cos (t)
onumber\]This is quadratic in cosine, so make one side 0 \[2\cos ^{2} (t)-\cos (t)-1=0
onumber\]Factor \[\left(2\cos (t)+1ight)\left(\cos (t)-1ight)=0
onumber\]Break this apart to solve each part separately \[2\cos (t)+1=0	ext{ or }\cos (t)-1=0
onumber\] \[\cos (t)=-\dfrac{1}{2}	ext{ or }\cos (t)=1
onumber\] \[t=\dfrac{2\pi }{3}	ext{ or }t=\dfrac{4\pi }{3}	ext{ or }t=0
onumber\] Looking at a graph of cos(2 t ) and cos( t ) shown together, we can verify that these three solutions on [0, 2 \(\pi\)) seem reasonable.

Example \(\PageIndex{6}\) A cannonball is fired with velocity of 100 meters per second. If it is launched at an angle of \(	heta\) , the vertical component of the velocity will be \(100\sin (	heta )\) and the horizontal component will be \(100\cos (	heta )\). Ignoring wind resistance, the height of the cannonball will follow the equation \(h(t)=-4.9t^{2} +100\sin (	heta )t\) and horizontal position will follow the equation \(x(t)=100\cos (	heta )t\). If you want to hit a target 900 meters away, at what angle should you aim the cannon? Solution To hit the target 900 meters away, we want \(x(t)=900\)at the time when the cannonball hits the ground, when \(h(t)=0\). To solve this problem, we will first solve for the time, \(t\), when the cannonball hits the ground. Our answer will depend upon the angle \(	heta\). \[h(t)=0
onumber\] \[-4.9t^{2} +100\sin (	heta )t=0
onumber\]Factor \[t\left(-4.9t+100\sin (	heta )ight)=0
onumber\]Break this apart to find two solutions \[t=0	ext{ or }-4.9t+100\sin (	heta )=0
onumber\] Solve for \(t\) \[-4.9t=-100\sin (	heta )
onumber\] \[t=\dfrac{100\sin (	heta )}{4.9}
onumber\] This shows that the height is 0 twice, once at \(t = 0\) when the cannonball is fired, and again when the cannonball hits the ground after flying through the air. This second value of \(t\) gives the time when the ball hits the ground in terms of the angle \(	heta\). We want the horizontal distance \(x(t)\) to be 900 when the ball hits the ground, in other words when \(t=\dfrac{100\sin (	heta )}{4.9}\). Since the target is 900 m away we start with \[x(t)=900
onumber\]Use the formula for \(x(t)\) \[100\cos (	heta )t=900
onumber\]Substitute the desired time, \(t\) from above \[100\cos (	heta )\dfrac{100\sin (	heta )}{4.9} =900
onumber\]Simplify \[\dfrac{100^{2} }{4.9} \cos (	heta )\sin (	heta )=900
onumber\]Isolate the cosine and sine product \[\cos (	heta )\sin (	heta )=\dfrac{900(4.9)}{100^{2} }
onumber\] The left side of this equation almost looks like the result of the double angle identity for sine: \[\sin (2	heta )=2\sin \left(	heta ight)\cos \left(	heta ight)
onumber\] Multiplying both sides of our equation by 2, \[2\cos (	heta )\sin (	heta )=\dfrac{2(900)(4.9)}{100^{2} }
onumber\]Using the double angle identity on the left \[\sin (2	heta )=\dfrac{2(900)(4.9)}{100^{2} }
onumber\]Use the inverse sine \[2	heta =\sin ^{-1} \left(\dfrac{2(900)(4.9)}{100^{2} } ight)\approx 1.080
onumber\]Divide by 2 \[	heta =\dfrac{1.080}{2} =0.540
onumber\], or about 30.94 degrees

topics.description: Two special cases of the sum of angles identities arise often enough that we choose to state these identities separately. IDENTITIES The double angle identities \[\sin (2\alpha )=2\sin (\alpha )\cos (\alpha )\] \[\begin{array}{ccc} {\cos (2\alpha )} & {=} & {\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\
topics.sample_content:
Two special cases of the sum of angles identities arise often enough that we choose to state these identities separately. IDENTITIES The double angle identities \[\sin (2\alpha )=2\sin (\alpha )\cos (\alpha )\] \[ egin{array}{ccc} {\cos (2\alpha )} & {=} & {\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )} \ {} & {=} & {1-2\sin ^{2} (\alpha )} \ {} & {=} & {2\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-1} \end{array}\] These identities follow from the sum of angles identities. Proof of the sine double angle identity \[\sin (2\alpha )
onumber\] \[=\sin (\alpha +\alpha )
onumber\]Apply the sum of angles identity \[=\sin (\alpha )\cos (\alpha )+\cos (\alpha )\sin (\alpha )
onumber\]Simplify \[=2\sin (\alpha )\cos (\alpha )
onumber\]Establishing the identity Exercise \(\PageIndex{1}\) Show \(\cos (2\alpha )=\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )\) by using the sum of angles identity for cosine. Answer \[ egin{array}{l} {\cos \left(2\alpha ight)=\cos (\alpha +\alpha )} \ {\cos (\alpha )\cos (\alpha )-\sin (\alpha )\sin (\alpha )} \ {\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )} \end{array}
onumber\] For the cosine double angle identity, there are three forms of the identity stated because the basic form, \(\cos (2\alpha )=\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )\), can be rewritten using the Pythagorean Identity. Rearranging the Pythagorean Identity results in the equality \(\cos ^{2} (\alpha )=1-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )\), and by substituting this into the basic double angle identity, we obtain the second form of the double angle identity. \[\cos (2\alpha )=\cos ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )
onumber\]Substituting using the Pythagorean identity \[\cos (2\alpha )=1-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )-\sin ^{2} (\alpha )
onumber\]Simplifying \[\cos (2\alpha )=1-2\sin ^{2} (\alpha )
onumber\] We can use the double angle identities to simplify expressions and prove identities. Exercise \(\PageIndex{2}\) Use an identity to find the exact value of \(\cos ^{2} \left(75{}^\circ ight)-\sin ^{2} \left(75{}^\circ ig

topics.title: Modeling Changing Amplitude and Midline
topics.body_text:
Section 7.4 Modeling Changing Amplitude and Midline While sinusoidal functions can model a variety of behaviors, it is often necessary to combine sinusoidal functions with linear and exponential curves to model real applications and behaviors. We begin this section by looking at changes to the midline of a sinusoidal function. Recall that the midline describes the middle, or average value, of the sinusoidal function. Changing Midlines This is an example of changing midline – in this case an exponentially changing midline. CHANGING MIDLINE A function of the form \(f(t)=A\sin (Bt)+g(t)\) will oscillate above and below the average given by the function \(g(t)\). Changing Midline Changing midlines can be exponential, linear, or any other type of function. Here are some examples: Exercise \(\PageIndex{1}\) Given the function \(g(x)=(x^{2} -1)+8\cos (x)\), describe the midline and amplitude using words. Answer The midline follows the path of the quadratic \(x^{2} -1\) and the amplitude is a constant value of 8. Changing Amplitude There are also situations in which the amplitude of a sinusoidal function does not stay constant. Back in Chapter 6, we modeled the motion of a spring using a sinusoidal function, but had to ignore friction in doing so. If there were friction in the system, we would expect the amplitude of the oscillation to decrease over time. In the equation \(f(t)=A\sin (Bt)+k\), \(A\) gives the amplitude of the oscillation, we can allow the amplitude to change by replacing this constant \(A\) with a function \(A(t)\). CHANGING AMPLITUDE A function of the form \(f(t)=A(t)\sin (Bt)+k\) will oscillate above and below the midline with an amplitude given by \(A(t)\). Here are some examples: When thinking about a spring with amplitude decreasing over time, it is tempting to use the simplest tool for the job – a lin ear function. But if we attempt to model the amplitude with a decreasing linear function, such as \(A(t)=10-t\), we quickly see the problem when we graph the equation \(f(t)=(10-t)\sin (4t)\). While the amplitude decreases at first as intended, the amplitude hits zero at \(t = 10\), then continues past the intercept, increasing in absolute value, which is not the expected behavior. This behavior and function may model the situation on a restricted domain and we might try to chalk the rest of it up to model breakdown, but in fact springs just don’t behave like this. A better model, as you will learn later in physics and calculus, would show the amplitude decreasing by a fixed percentage each second, leading to an exponential decay model for the amplitude. DAMPED HARMONIC MOTION Damped harmonic motion , exhibited by springs subject to friction, follows a model of the form \[f(t)=ab^{t} \sin (Bt)+k	ext{ or }f(t)=ae^{rt} \sin (Bt)+k
onumber\] Exercise \(\PageIndex{2}\) A certain stock started at a high value of $7 per share, oscillating monthly above and below the average value, with the oscillation decreasing by 2% per year. However, the average value started at $4 per share and has grown linearly by 50 cents per year. a. Find a formula for the midline and the amplitude. b. Find a function \(S(t)\) that models the value of the stock after \(t\) years. Answer \[ egin{array}{l} {m(t)=4+0.5t} \ {A(t)=7(0.98)^{t} } \end{array}
onumber\] \[S(t)= 7(0.98)^{t} \cos \left(24\pi tight)+4+0.5t
onumber\] Important Topics of This Section Changing midline Changing amplitude Linear Changes Exponential Changes Damped Harmonic Motion While sinusoidal functions can model a variety of behaviors, it is often necessary to combine sinusoidal functions with linear and exponential curves to model real applications and behaviors. We begin this section by looking at changes to the midline of a sinusoidal function. Recall that the midline describes the middle, or average value, of the sinusoidal function. Changing Midlines This is an example of changing midline – in this case an exponentially changing midline. CHANGING MIDLINE A function of the form \(f(t)=A\sin (Bt)+g(t)\) will oscillate above and below the average given by the function \(g(t)\). Changing Midline Changing midlines can be exponential, linear, or any other type of function. Here are some examples: Exercise \(\PageIndex{1}\) Given the function \(g(x)=(x^{2} -1)+8\cos (x)\), describe the midline and amplitude using words. The midline follows the path of the quadratic \(x^{2} -1\) and the amplitude is a constant value of 8. Changing Amplitude There are also situations in which the amplitude of a sinusoidal function does not stay constant. Back in Chapter 6, we modeled the motion of a spring using a sinusoidal function, but had to ignore friction in doing so. If there were friction in the system, we would expect the amplitude of the oscillation to decrease over time. In the equation \(f(t)=A\sin (Bt)+k\), \(A\) gives the amplitude of the oscillation, we can allow the amplitude to change by replacing this constant \(A\) with a function \(A(t)\). CHANGING AMPLITUDE A function of the form \(f(t)=A(t)\sin (Bt)+k\) will oscillate above and below the midline with an amplitude given by \(A(t)\). Here are some examples: When thinking about a spring with amplitude decreasing over time, it is tempting to use the simplest tool for the job – a lin ear function. But if we attempt to model the amplitude with a decreasing linear function, such as \(A(t)=10-t\), we quickly see the problem when we graph the equation \(f(t)=(10-t)\sin (4t)\). While the amplitude decreases at first as intended, the amplitude hits zero at \(t = 10\), then continues past the intercept, increasing in absolute value, which is not the expected behavior. This behavior and function may model the situation on a restricted domain and we might try to chalk the rest of it up to model breakdown, but in fact springs just don’t behave like this. A better model, as you will learn later in physics and calculus, would show the amplitude decreasing by a fixed percentage each second, leading to an exponential decay model for the amplitude. DAMPED HARMONIC MOTION Damped harmonic motion , exhibited by springs subject to friction, follows a model of the form \[f(t)=ab^{t} \sin (Bt)+k	ext{ or }f(t)=ae^{rt} \sin (Bt)+k
onumber\] Exercise \(\PageIndex{2}\) A certain stock started at a high value of $7 per share, oscillating monthly above and below the average value, with the oscillation decreasing by 2% per year. However, the average value started at $4 per share and has grown linearly by 50 cents per year. a. Find a formula for the midline and the amplitude. b. Find a function \(S(t)\) that models the value of the stock after \(t\) years. \[ egin{array}{l} {m(t)=4+0.5t} \ {A(t)=7(0.98)^{t} } \end{array}
onumber\] \[S(t)= 7(0.98)^{t} \cos \left(24\pi tight)+4+0.5t
onumber\]

examples.text:
Example \(\PageIndex{2}\) Find a function with linear midline of the form \(f(t)=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+mt+b\) that will pass through the points given below. \(t\) 0 1 2 3 \(f(t)\) 5 10 9 8 Since we are given the value of the horizontal compression coefficient we can calculate the period of this function: new period = \(\dfrac{	ext{original period}}{B}\) = \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/2}\) = 4. Solution Since the sine function is at the midline at the beginning of a cycle and halfway through a cycle, we would expect this function to be at the midline at t = 0 and t = 2, since 2 is half the full period of 4. Based on this, we expect the points (0, 5) and (2, 9) to be points on the midline. We can clearly see that this is not a constant function and so we use the two points to calculate a linear function: \(midline=mt+b\). From these two points we can calculate a slope: \[m=\dfrac{9-5}{2-0} =\dfrac{4}{2} =2
onumber\] Combining this with the initial value of 5, we have the midline: \(midline=2t+5\). The full function will have form \(f(t)=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+2t+5\). To find the amplitude, we can plug in a point we haven’t already used, such as (1, 10). \[10=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (1)ight)+2(1)+5
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and combine like terms \[10=A+7
onumber\] \[A=3
onumber\] A function of the form given fitting the data would be \[f(t)=3\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+2t+5
onumber\] Notice we could have taken an alternate approach by plugging points (0, 5) and (2, 9) into the original equation. Substituting (0, 5), \[5=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (0)ight)+m(0)+b
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and simplify \[5=b
onumber\] Substituting (2, 9) \[9=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (2)ight)+m(2)+5
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and simplify \[9=2m+5
onumber\] \[4=2m
onumber\] \[m=2
onumber\]as we found above. Now we can proceed to find \(A\) the same way we did before.

Example \(\PageIndex{3}\) The number of tourists visiting a ski and hiking resort averages 4000 people annually and oscillates seasonally, 1000 above and below the average. Due to a marketing campaign, the average number of tourists has been increasing by 200 each year. Write an equation for the number of tourists after \(t\) years, beginning at the peak season. Solution Again there are two components to this problem: the oscillation and the average. For the oscillation, the number of tourists oscillates 1000 above and below average, giving an amplitude of 1000. Since the oscillation is seasonal, it has a period of 1 year. Since we are given a starting point of “peak season”, we will model this scenario with a cosine function. So far, this gives an equation in the form \(N(t)=1000\cos (2\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+midline\). The average is currently 4000, and is increasing by 200 each year. This is a constant rate of change, so this is linear growth, \(average=4000+200t\). This function will act as the midline. Combining these two pieces gives a function for the number of tourists: \[N(t)=1000\cos (2\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+4000+200t\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{4}\) A spring with natural length of feet inches is pulled back 6 feet and released. It oscillates once every 2 seconds. Its amplitude decreases by 20% each second. Find a function that models the position of the spring t seconds after being released. Solution Since the spring will oscillate on either side of the natural length, the midline will be at 20 feet. The oscillation has a period of 2 seconds, and so the horizontal compression coefficient is \(B=\pi\). Additionally, it begins at the furthest distance from the wall, indicating a cosine model. Meanwhile, the amplitude begins at 6 feet, and decreases by 20% each second, giving an amplitude function of \(A(t)=6(1-0.20)^{t}\). Combining this with the sinusoidal information gives a function for the position of the spring: \[f(t)=6(0.80)^{t} \cos (\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+20\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{6}\) In AM (Amplitude Modulated) radio, a carrier wave with a high frequency is used to transmit music or other signals by applying the to-be-transmitted signal as the amplitude of the carrier signal. A musical note with frequency 110 Hz (Hertz = cycles per second) is to be carried on a wave with frequency of 2 KHz (KiloHertz = thousands of cycles per second). If the musical wave has an amplitude of 3, write a function describing the broadcast wave. Solution The carrier wave, with a frequency of 2000 cycles per second, would have period \(\dfrac{1}{2000}\) of a second, giving an equation of the form \(\sin (4000\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\). Our choice of a sine function here was arbitrary – it would have worked just was well to use a cosine. The musical tone, with a frequency of 110 cycles per second, would have a period of \(\dfrac{1}{110}\) of a second. With an amplitude of 3, this would correspond to a function of the form \(3\sin (220\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\). Again our choice of using a sine function is arbitrary. The musical wave is acting as the amplitude of the carrier wave, so we will multiply the musical tone’s function by the carrier wave function, resulting in the function \[f(t)=3\sin (220\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\sin (4000\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{2}\) Find a function with linear midline of the form \(f(t)=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+mt+b\) that will pass through the points given below. \(t\) 0 1 2 3 \(f(t)\) 5 10 9 8 Since we are given the value of the horizontal compression coefficient we can calculate the period of this function: new period = \(\dfrac{	ext{original period}}{B}\) = \(\dfrac{2\pi}{\pi/2}\) = 4. Solution Since the sine function is at the midline at the beginning of a cycle and halfway through a cycle, we would expect this function to be at the midline at t = 0 and t = 2, since 2 is half the full period of 4. Based on this, we expect the points (0, 5) and (2, 9) to be points on the midline. We can clearly see that this is not a constant function and so we use the two points to calculate a linear function: \(midline=mt+b\). From these two points we can calculate a slope: \[m=\dfrac{9-5}{2-0} =\dfrac{4}{2} =2
onumber\] Combining this with the initial value of 5, we have the midline: \(midline=2t+5\). The full function will have form \(f(t)=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+2t+5\). To find the amplitude, we can plug in a point we haven’t already used, such as (1, 10). \[10=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (1)ight)+2(1)+5
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and combine like terms \[10=A+7
onumber\] \[A=3
onumber\] A function of the form given fitting the data would be \[f(t)=3\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} tight)+2t+5
onumber\] Notice we could have taken an alternate approach by plugging points (0, 5) and (2, 9) into the original equation. Substituting (0, 5), \[5=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (0)ight)+m(0)+b
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and simplify \[5=b
onumber\] Substituting (2, 9) \[9=A\sin \left(\dfrac{\pi }{2} (2)ight)+m(2)+5
onumber\]Evaluate the sine and simplify \[9=2m+5
onumber\] \[4=2m
onumber\] \[m=2
onumber\]as we found above. Now we can proceed to find \(A\) the same way we did before.

Example \(\PageIndex{3}\) The number of tourists visiting a ski and hiking resort averages 4000 people annually and oscillates seasonally, 1000 above and below the average. Due to a marketing campaign, the average number of tourists has been increasing by 200 each year. Write an equation for the number of tourists after \(t\) years, beginning at the peak season. Solution Again there are two components to this problem: the oscillation and the average. For the oscillation, the number of tourists oscillates 1000 above and below average, giving an amplitude of 1000. Since the oscillation is seasonal, it has a period of 1 year. Since we are given a starting point of “peak season”, we will model this scenario with a cosine function. So far, this gives an equation in the form \(N(t)=1000\cos (2\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+midline\). The average is currently 4000, and is increasing by 200 each year. This is a constant rate of change, so this is linear growth, \(average=4000+200t\). This function will act as the midline. Combining these two pieces gives a function for the number of tourists: \[N(t)=1000\cos (2\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+4000+200t\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{4}\) A spring with natural length of feet inches is pulled back 6 feet and released. It oscillates once every 2 seconds. Its amplitude decreases by 20% each second. Find a function that models the position of the spring t seconds after being released. Solution Since the spring will oscillate on either side of the natural length, the midline will be at 20 feet. The oscillation has a period of 2 seconds, and so the horizontal compression coefficient is \(B=\pi\). Additionally, it begins at the furthest distance from the wall, indicating a cosine model. Meanwhile, the amplitude begins at 6 feet, and decreases by 20% each second, giving an amplitude function of \(A(t)=6(1-0.20)^{t}\). Combining this with the sinusoidal information gives a function for the position of the spring: \[f(t)=6(0.80)^{t} \cos (\pi {\kern 1pt} t)+20\nonumber\]

Example \(\PageIndex{6}\) In AM (Amplitude Modulated) radio, a carrier wave with a high frequency is used to transmit music or other signals by applying the to-be-transmitted signal as the amplitude of the carrier signal. A musical note with frequency 110 Hz (Hertz = cycles per second) is to be carried on a wave with frequency of 2 KHz (KiloHertz = thousands of cycles per second). If the musical wave has an amplitude of 3, write a function describing the broadcast wave. Solution The carrier wave, with a frequency of 2000 cycles per second, would have period \(\dfrac{1}{2000}\) of a second, giving an equation of the form \(\sin (4000\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\). Our choice of a sine function here was arbitrary – it would have worked just was well to use a cosine. The musical tone, with a frequency of 110 cycles per second, would have a period of \(\dfrac{1}{110}\) of a second. With an amplitude of 3, this would correspond to a function of the form \(3\sin (220\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\). Again our choice of using a sine function is arbitrary. The musical wave is acting as the amplitude of the carrier wave, so we will multiply the musical tone’s function by the carrier wave function, resulting in the function \[f(t)=3\sin (220\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\sin (4000\pi {\kern 1pt} t)\nonumber\]
